event: Spanish navigator Juan de Bermúdez sighted the uninhabited islands, which were later named Bermuda after him.
Juan de Bermúdez and chronicler Gonzalo Fernández de Oviedo visited again, leaving the first detailed description of the islands.
An inscribed rock carving (the 'Spanish Rock'), bearing the date and initials of a shipwrecked sailor, recorded one of the earliest human marks on the islands.
The English Virginia Company ship Sea Venture, bound for Jamestown, wrecked on Bermuda's reefs, stranding Admiral Sir George Somers and around 150 survivors who founded the first settlement.
Most survivors departed for Jamestown aboard two locally built ships, the Deliverance and Patience, leaving a small party behind to hold the islands for England.
The Virginia Company formally took control and sent the ship Plough with about 60 settlers, and the town of St. George's was established as the capital.
Administration passed to the Somers Isles Company, a spin-off of the Virginia Company chartered specifically to govern Bermuda.
The first enslaved Africans and Native Americans were brought to Bermuda, beginning a long history of slavery on the islands.
Bermuda's House of Assembly met for the first time, making it one of the oldest continuous parliaments in the Western Hemisphere.
The Somers Isles Company's charter was revoked and Bermuda became a Crown colony governed directly by the English monarchy.
Following the Acts of Union, Bermuda became a colony of the new Kingdom of Great Britain.
During the American Revolution, Bermudians secretly traded their gunpowder to the rebellious colonies in exchange for desperately needed food supplies (the 'Gunpowder Plot').
Bermuda's first newspaper, the Bermuda Gazette, began publication in St. George's.
The British Royal Navy began surveying and developing facilities, leading to the establishment of a major naval dockyard on Ireland Island.
The capital was moved from St. George's to the more central town of Hamilton.
Slavery was abolished throughout the British Empire, emancipating Bermuda's enslaved population on 1 August.
During the American Civil War, St. George's flourished as a base for blockade-runners smuggling goods to the Confederacy.
Boer War prisoners captured by the British were interned in camps on islands in Bermuda's Great Sound.
Bermuda celebrated the tercentenary of its parliament, underscoring its long colonial self-governing tradition.
The first regular trans-Atlantic seaplane and steamship tourism boom transformed Bermuda into a fashionable winter resort destination.
The Bermuda Railway opened, providing a rail line running the length of the islands.
Commercial flying-boat air service to Bermuda began, opening the islands to faster international travel.
Under a wartime agreement, the United States established military bases in Bermuda, including a major air and naval station, in exchange for destroyers lent to Britain.
Women who met property qualifications gained the right to vote in Bermuda's elections.
Bermuda banned private automobiles for decades; this year marked the lifting of that long-standing prohibition, permitting cars on the islands.
The Bermuda Railway ceased operations and its equipment was sold to British Guiana.
Bermuda hosted the 'Big Three' summit conference between the leaders of the United States, Britain, and France.
A peaceful boycott of segregated cinemas, the Theatre Boycott, led to the desegregation of public accommodations across the islands.
Universal adult suffrage was effectively introduced as Bermuda's first political parties formed, including the Progressive Labour Party.
The United Bermuda Party was founded, becoming the dominant governing party for the next several decades.
Bermuda adopted a new constitution granting internal self-government with a premier and cabinet, while Britain retained responsibility for defence and foreign affairs.
Sir Edward Richards became Bermuda's first Black government leader, taking the title of premier.
Governor Sir Richard Sharples and his aide were assassinated at Government House, triggering a period of unrest.
Riots erupted after the execution of two men convicted of the governor's murder, prompting the deployment of British troops.
A general strike paralyzed the islands as labour disputes highlighted ongoing economic and social tensions.
Voters rejected independence from the United Kingdom in a referendum, and the United States closed its military bases on the islands.
The Progressive Labour Party won its first general election, ending decades of United Bermuda Party rule and bringing the first PLP government to power.
Dame Jennifer Smith was replaced by Alex Scott as premier, and Hurricane Fabian struck Bermuda, causing fatalities and extensive damage.
Bermuda agreed to accept several Uyghur detainees released from the U.S. Guantanamo Bay prison, drawing international attention.
The One Bermuda Alliance won the general election, returning a coalition government to power.
Hamilton Harbour hosted the lead-up to the 35th America's Cup, with the prestigious sailing event boosting the economy.
Bermuda hosted the 35th America's Cup sailing races, and the Progressive Labour Party returned to government under Premier David Burt; a controversy over same-sex marriage legislation also unfolded.
Bermuda passed the Domestic Partnership Act, controversially replacing same-sex marriage with civil partnerships after courts had earlier permitted such marriages.
Bermuda marked the 410th anniversary of continuous English settlement as one of Britain's oldest overseas territories.
